source: Web search results - CFPB enforcement and regulatory signals

zelle_lawsuit.allegation: Allowing fraud to fester on Zelle

violations: Directed users to ask banks to reverse transactions, then denied those reversals
Suppressed users from seeking help to reduce costs

senate_letter.request: Urge CFPB to engage in notice-and-comment rulemaking regarding what counts as an authorized or unauthorized transfer under Regulation E
senate_letter.context: Consumer payment apps have exposed gaps in existing rules

digital_wallet_supervision_rule.market_share: 98% of nonbank general-use digital payment market

reg_e_authorized_transactions.description: EFTA does not require banks to reimburse for APP fraud since payments are technically authorized - even if obtained through deception
reg_e_authorized_transactions.source: Federal Reserve Bank of Atlanta, September 2024

current_legal_framework.bank_defense: Banks argue consumers are responsible for ensuring legitimacy of recipients
current_legal_framework.consumer_advocate_position: Financial institutions should take more responsibility given sophisticated nature of modern scams

uk_comparison.policy: Mandatory reimbursement for APP fraud victims
uk_comparison.effective: October 2024 under Financial Services Markets Act 2023
uk_comparison.relevance: US lawmakers may look to UK model

key_insight: The 'authorized transaction' defense is under regulatory and legislative pressure. CFPB has sued Zelle and fined Cash App $175M. Senators are pushing for Reg E rulemaking.
